text: Code refactoring is the process of improving the internal structure of code without changing how it behaves. For example, you might break a long function into smaller parts or rename unclear variables. Refactoring doesn't add new features; instead, it makes the code easier to read, understand, and maintain. Over time, code can become messy or overly complex—this is called "technical debt." If you don't fix it, future changes become slower and riskier. Regular refactoring helps reduce technical debt, keeps the code clean, and saves time in the long run. Good code is not only functional but also clear and organized.
